crossReferences.takeaway: Present content items in a horizontal-scroll film-strip row with each card showing a cinematic still, and use dramatic dark backgrounds with spotlight text treatment for section headers.

influences.description: The Now Showing section uses a horizontal-scroll film-strip row where each project card has a full-bleed gradient poster art area. The cards scroll horizontally with snap alignment, directly mirroring A24's filmography browsing pattern.

The featured film hero card uses a dramatic dark gradient background (linear-gradient from deep purple to near-black) with a projection-glow radial gradient overlay. The title, tagline, and metadata are overlaid in cinematic title-card style with generous padding.

All section headings use spotlight text treatment — Righteous font in neon yellow with text-shadow glow against the deep purple background, creating the effect of marquee lettering lit against darkness.

crossReferences.takeaway: Use a 'now showing' hero pattern: one featured item gets the full marquee treatment (large image, editorial blurb, countdown/date) while supporting items queue below in categorized rows.

influences.description: The dashboard features a single 'NOW SHOWING' hero card for The Neon Paradox with full marquee treatment: large backdrop, editorial tagline ('A neo-noir thriller set in a city that never sleeps...'), genre badges, and a countdown badge ('Wrapping in 12 days') that creates temporal urgency just like MUBI's 'leaving in X days' pattern.

The projects view is split into 'NOW SHOWING' (large horizontal film-strip cards for active productions) and 'COMING SOON' (smaller poster grid for upcoming films). This directly mirrors MUBI's categorized carousel approach with curatorial headlines.

The 'NOW SHOWING' badge that tops the hero card serves as the curatorial headline, acting as a tab-like header that establishes the section's temporal context — what's actively happening versus what's queued.

crossReferences.takeaway: Assign each content item a visible sequential catalog number (like Criterion spine numbers) displayed prominently, creating a collector's index that implies completeness and curation.

influences.description: Every content item across the entire application has a visible catalog number styled in Source Code Pro monospace font. Projects use '#SP-XXXX' (Starlight Pictures catalog), tasks use '#TK-XXX', dailies use '#DL-XXX', and pitches use '#PITCH-XXX'. These are displayed in a muted but consistent style, creating a collector's index feel throughout.

Each pitch on the Pitch Wall shows its catalog number (#PITCH-012 through #PITCH-017) in the poster art area, mirroring how Criterion spine numbers appear on physical covers. The sequential numbering implies a curated collection of ideas that has been built over time.

The schema planner uses a film credits format where each entity is displayed with a role, dotted connecting line, and name — similar to how Criterion gives editorial essays and supplementary material equal visual weight alongside the primary content. The credits format treats production relationships as first-class data.
